So, as you can see in the top common holdings, we have dividend income there (you can scroll on the table to look at the additional columns). The max port value currently is Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) at 1.75% of the portfolio. The equity values will rise and fall week to week based on the market and the sector. But in addition to the common stock dividends, where the income can move is the ELNs. Here is the deal, folks: the volatility of late is great for the portion of the portfolio in the ELNs. You see, since JEPI is essentially selling these options (via the ELNs), it collects more money when volatility is high. This is why you’ll often see JEPI’s monthly dividend spike during months when the market is swinging wildly, as the fear in the market is literally being converted into cash. As such, we expect some higher payouts are in the cards thanks to recent activity, likely with the next announced distribution. The most recently announced distribution was this week, $0.3513 for the month, and was paid today (the time of this writing). Since February was a bit more volatile, this was higher than the $0.3444 the month prior. We suspect April will be higher so long as this volatility continues. But of course, the distribution does fluctuate, so market conditions will dictate moves in the payout.

Make no mistake, the market for two years, outside of a few smaller corrections and the March and April 2025 selloff from tariffs, volatility has been low. What do you notice above in 2025? Payouts spiked in the spring of 2025 due to more volatility. Well, we have been volatile, so that is why higher payouts are in the cards. For the long-term investor, the math is actually pretty compelling. If you do some simple math on the current yield, you could theoretically recoup your entire initial principal investment in about 13 years just through these monthly distributions alone, assuming the yield holds firm. Our approach at BAD BEAT Investing is to blend these kinds of income plays with growth and value positions, often converting short-term double-digit profits into lifetime house positions, while income-generating machines like JEPI provide added cash flow for more investment. We still like the name as a hold right now, but the best way to play it is either through consistent monthly dollar-cost averaging or by waiting for those sharp market dips to build your position at a lower cost basis. While capital appreciation is a bonus, and we have seen a steady climb for about a year, we view this as a buy and hold for the cash flow.

data.title: DGS: The Purest Emerging Markets Small-Cap Value ETF
data.description:
Intro

From a fund strategy standpoint, ETF.com had a great summary of The WisdomTree Emerging Markets SmallCap Dividend Fund ETF (DGS): It tracks a dividend-weighted index of small-cap, dividend-paying emerging market stocks. It holds emerging market companies that have paid dividends over the past calendar year and weights them based on the size of dividends paid. DGS only includes stocks that are in the bottom 10% of the total market cap of the WisdomTree Emerging Markets Dividend Index. The resulting portfolio is a proxy for an Emerging Market Small Cap Value asset class (EM SCV).

You typically see EM SCV language pop up when building a factor-based portfolio that "tilts" to a certain asset class. In a previous article, I explained why this may be a good idea. The performance speaks for itself.

But in this article, I want to focus on the performance of DGS compared to other similar funds. While it has actually underperformed compared to peers, I actually think it's still a great time to buy simply because of the concentrated factor loadings in the small-cap space. To me, DGS is a better option for the small cap value emerging market ("EM SCV") space. Although total risk-adjusted returns, expenses, and turnover have lagged competitor funds in recent years, factor regression statistics are still in favor for DGS.

DGS vs Peers

Some funds to compare DGS to would be the Avantis Emerging Markets Equity ETF (AVEM) and the Avantis Emerging Markets Value ETF (AVES). I'll get into the key differences later in the article, but these are some well-respected emerging market funds that are used to target emerging markets in a globally diversified portfolio. Performance, however, has dragged for DGS the last 3 years, with DGS at a 48.16% total return versus 60.72% for AVES and 70.38% for AVEM.

And from a risk standpoint, DGS is a bit more concentrated in number of holdings, due to its tighter strategy, but the least concentrated in top 10 overweight. Turnover is a bit high, and holdings for DGS are pretty diversified across several sectors as well.

From a fund summary standpoint, DGS is a bit older than the others with a slightly higher expense ratio due to its strategy. It also is slightly more liquid than AVES, but the most popular fund is clearly AVEM, however.

Overall, this comparison does not put DGS in favor. It has underperformed, tax inefficient turnover, and is more expensive. There is one thing DGS has going for it however, and that is the factor statistics revealing it's a true EM SCV fund.

Regression

If one is specifically trying to target EM SCV, perhaps for a barbell approach with large caps, DGS is the superior choice due to its favorable size factor loading. While the other similar funds have outperformed, they are technically in different ballgames.

For example, DGS size loading ("SMB") is -0.22 where AVES is -0.32 and AVEM -0.41. While on the surface, this doesn't seem like much, the orders of magnitude relative to each other are drastic. Taking a ratio of these means DGS size loading is around 31% smaller than AVES and 47% smaller than AVEM. In other words, DGS is comprised of much smaller companies, whereas the other could be more categorized as mid-cap, at least by factor loadings. DGS and AVES value loadings ("HML") are also twice as good as AVEM. Comparatively, DGS is a true EM SCV fund, AVES is more of a EM MCV fund, and AVEM is just a EM fund. So if one is to target the EM SCV asset classes, DGS is a winner, with AVES a close second.

Conclusion

AVES has outperformed DGS on every metric, so why would we even entertain DGS?

The answer lies in its factor loadings and a mean regression of the small-cap premium. DGS offers one of the purest exposures to the emerging markets small-cap value factor currently available. While AVES and AVEM have delivered stronger recent returns, their factor exposures suggest they sit higher in the market cap spectrum. For investors specifically targeting EM small-cap value, DGS remains the closest proxy. Factor investing is about maintaining exposure to compensated risk factors over long horizons and not chasing recent winners. If the small-cap premium in emerging markets reasserts itself, funds with deeper exposure to that factor, like DGS, should be positioned to benefit the most. I am long DGS in my personal globally diversified factor-tilted portfolio with a 10% weight and rebalancing.

Additional Considerations

Though this is more of a comparison article, I wanted to briefly touch on the state of the EM SCV state as a whole and why someone might overweight this asset class even after the 30-40% run-up.

From 1995-2026 EM delivered a 6.43% CAGR on a 0.29 Sharpe ratio due to very large volatility swings. Over the same timeframe, the US stock market delivered a 10.88% return on a 0.59 Sharpe. In other words, EM has underperformed on a return basis and nearly half the risk-adjusted return to the US market. Believers in highly efficient markets are watering at the mouth here since, under such conditions, all sound asset classes should deliver similar risk-adjusted returns over the long run. This means either US stocks must underperform to match EM or EM must vastly outperform. We've seen something similar from 1994 to 2007, and mean reversion is looking juicy here.

I'm not the only one who thinks this as well. AQR recently published expected returns for different market listings, with EM outperforming the US by a magnitude of nearly 20%.

Although I think DGS is better to target the small cap space, which evidence has shown is due to a mean reversion, any of the listed funds would be a great addition to a globally diversified portfolio.

data.title: OAKM: Actively Managed Value ETF Following A Questionable Strategy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In a Christmas Eve interview on CNBC's "Squawk On The Street", Bill Nygren, portfolio manager of the Oakmark U.S. Large Cap ETF (OAKM), noted the challenge active managers faced in 2025, where, unless they were overweight the mega-cap growth stocks, it was difficult to keep up with passive indices. Nygren also noted the unusual strength of the momentum factor that year and indicated his belief that some form of mean reversion will occur this year. With OAKM, Nygren has put his money where his mouth is by selecting stocks with significantly weak price momentum, and while that strategy could work well, it introduces shareholders to significant earnings variability and concentration risks. As a result, I'm uneasy about OAKM's composition, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

OAKM Overview

According to its website, investors should consider OAKM for three reasons:

1. Access to an experienced large-cap value investment team led by Bill Nygren, Michael Nicolas, and Robert Bierig.

2. Consistent investment philosophy that identifies quality companies priced at a discount to our estimate of intrinsic value.

3. Active management in an exchange-traded fund structure that provides transparency, intraday liquidity, and the potential for tax-efficient investing.

Estimates of intrinsic value depend on the manager, so that doesn't really tell us much. However, I wanted to see how the team defined "quality", and I found that in the fund's prospectus, the advisor looks for companies with solid free cash flows and earnings that are growing and reasonably predictable. In addition, the advisor looks for a "high level of company management alignment with shareholders," which, based on the description below, relates to stock ownership percentages and performance-based compensation.

Unfortunately, the prospectus makes no mention of traditional quality metrics (e.g., margins, capital efficiency ratios) and only outlines management's general "bottom-up" approach to stock selection. Therefore, I'll assess OAKM's quality independently later in this article compared to its two benchmarks, represented by the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD) and the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

Lastly, key fund characteristics for OAKM include a 0.59% expense ratio, $1.08 billion in assets under management, and a December 3, 2024, inception date. That means OAKM's history is short and likely not too meaningful, but the team still has a good long-term track record of outperforming the S&P 500 Index with the Oakmark Fund (OAKMX). As shown below, despite struggling over the last few years, OAKMX has outperformed by 2% per year since its inception in August 1991.

OAKM Performance Analysis

From January 2025 through February 2026, OAKM has gained an annualized 17.96%, compared to 20.27% and 15.60% for IWD and SPY, respectively. You can see in the chart below that OAKM jumped out to an early lead but saw its growth flatten in mid-2025 and has more or less matched SPY's returns in the first two months of 2026. Meanwhile, IWD is up 7.22% YTD, clearly a major benefactor of the market's rotation to value.

One likely explanation is that OAKM is highly concentrated in the Financials sector, with exposure currently sitting around 33%, down from about 38% in January 2025. As a proxy, consider that the Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) is down 6.10% YTD, so at least in the short term, the fund faces this sector headwind. However, its above-average exposure to Energy (14%) is proving beneficial after the U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran. Crude oil futures are up to $74 per barrel at the time of writing, or about 29% YTD.

Earnings Variability Risk

Regardless of your outlook on Financials and Energy , I want to emphasize the earnings variability risk introduced by overweighting these two sectors. Using S&P 500 Index operating earnings per share data for each sector over the short term (last 12 quarters) and the long-term (last 40 quarters), I calculated coefficients of variations, or CVs, by dividing the standard deviation of EPS by the average over the respective period. As shown below, Energy's was by far the highest over the long term (108.6%), while Financials (37.1%) comes in second place behind Technology (42.5%).

I also calculated earnings variability scores for each ETF by weighting their sector allocations by these short- and long-term CVs, and OAKM ranked #135/149 among all the large-cap value ETFs I track. Therefore, you should expect OAKM's returns to be more volatile for the foreseeable future. In uncertain markets, I look to reduce risk, so I view that feature negatively.

OAKM Fundamental Analysis

In my investment thesis, I noted Nygren's belief that the momentum factor would lose some of its strength this year. To a certain extent, I share that sentiment, but I also don't think it's right to buy stocks simply because they're underperforming. Mean reversion isn't somehow programmed into the market, and when I look at OAKM, I see a portfolio that barely beat EPS expectations last quarter (+2.35%). I also see a portfolio that has, on average, somewhere between a "C" and "C+" EPS Revision Score from Seeking Alpha. For context, that ranks #136/149 in its category compared to #66/149 for IWD.

I suggest weak earnings surprises and revisions are good reasons for OAKM's current selections underperforming. As shown above, OAKM's holdings are up only 7.38% and 10.52% on a weighted average basis over the last six months and one year compared to 24.18% and 36.74% for IWD, so I'm not convinced management is exploiting any market inefficiencies. Furthermore, OAKM's quality metrics (EBIT margins and ROE) are below its benchmarks, which I view as a missed opportunity. OAKM managers should be able to improve a passive benchmark's quality features because they can be extremely selective by choosing only 30-40 stocks rather than several hundred.

Extremely Cheap Value: OAKM's True Strength

However, it seems as though management has focused its efforts on finding stocks that trade at extremely cheap valuations. To illustrate, consider that OAKM only trades at 13.42x forward earnings right now, which represents a 27% and 39% discount to IWD and SPY, respectively. The discount is even bigger on a trailing price-to-book and trailing price-to-cash flow, and trying to capitalize on these huge gaps is something that makes sense to me.

It also makes sense if some of its top holdings can deliver on earnings growth expectations. For example, analysts project about 27% EPS growth for Phillips 66 (PSX) and Citigroup Inc. (C) over the next twelve months, which would be a huge improvement compared to their -26.47% and -1.59% annualized growth rates over the last three years. At the portfolio level, OAKM's next-year EPS growth rate is 10.96%, which is excellent from a GARP perspective, given its 13.42x forward P/E ratio. Still, the risk is that much of this expected growth comes from stocks in the Financials and Energy sectors which, as we know from earlier, aren't always reliable. To further illustrate, consider the expected EPS growth rates for these stocks as of March 1, 2025, compared to what they actually delivered over the last year:

Ticker Company Name 1Y EPS Growth Estimate (March 2025) Actual 1Y EPS Growth Difference
COP ConocoPhillips 1.12% -18.69% -19.81%
PSX Phillips 66 -8.29% 116.14% 124.43%
C Citigroup Inc. 32.80% 17.64% -15.16%
KDP Keurig Dr. Pepper Inc. 6.67% 45.71% 39.04%
TRGP Targa Resources Corp. 36.51% 47.91% 11.40%
MRK Merck & Co., Inc. 87.35% 8.01% -79.34%
STT State Street Corporation 12.21% 14.49% 2.29%
DE Deere & Co. -13.42% -21.56% -8.14%
ICE Intercontinental Exchange, Inc. 10.32% 20.71% 10.39%
AIG American International Group, Inc. 4.47% 62.25% 57.78%

Notice how estimates were wildly inaccurate for Energy and Financial stocks like ConocoPhillips, Phillips 66, and Citigroup, underscoring these sectors' general unpredictability. That's not to say positive results can't happen for OAKM, but I question why so much of the portfolio's value proposition is tied to stocks in these sectors. To me, it seems unnecessarily aggressive, especially given the positioning of other active value funds. The SEI Enhanced Large Cap Value Factor ETF (SEIV) comes to mind. It has a combined 19% in Energy and Financials and also has the edge over OAKM on estimated EPS growth (14.00% vs. 10.96%), forward P/E (11.63x vs. 13.42x), ROE (20.42% vs. 16.27%), and last quarter earnings surprises (7.17% vs. 2.35%). Simply put, I believe the odds of success are greater with SEIV, and it doesn't hurt that its expense ratio is much lower (0.15% vs. 0.59%).

Investment Recommendation

OAKM is an actively managed large-cap value ETF led by three experienced portfolio managers with a solid record of outperforming the market over the long run. Currently, the portfolio is priced cheaply, trading at just 13.42x forward earnings and 8.97x trailing cash flow, and if consensus double-digit earnings growth expectations materialize, buying OAKM today would be an excellent play from a GARP perspective.

However, my view is that OAKM management is taking an unnecessary risk by assigning so much to stocks in the Financials and Energy sectors, whose EPS variability is high and challenging to predict. Furthermore, many of OAKM's selections have negative earnings revisions and weak earnings surprises, and I suggest those as logical reasons for why they've lagged the market over the last year as opposed to any irrational market mispricing. My analysis also revealed only average quality, a disappointing find for a 30-40 stock fund, and from a pure factor perspective, I think there are better options. This article identified SEIV as an alternative, which I invite you to read about here.

Normally, I would assign OAKM a "sell" rating based on its unattractive factor mix, but I've decided to initiate a neutral "hold" instead in recognition of the team's long-term success. After all, it is an active fund, and I expect its managers will pivot if the current weak-momentum strategy doesn't work out. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to your questions afterward.
